story:
These days, I can’t make myself get out of bed unless I know coffee is waiting. On vacations, I set my alarm for when the nearest coffee shop opens. I still end up rolling over because it’s too far away—how am I supposed to shower, get dressed, dry my hair, and do my makeup without caffeine? Even so, I call myself a Morning Person. I’m at my best from 5 to 9 a.m.—if I can wrap my hands around a hot mug. I like the bitter bite softened with collagen creamer or oat milk. Heavy cream tastes best and makes me feel like I’m back at my parents’ house during the holidays, but it always breaks me out along the jawline. When I do my Morning Pages in the dark before sunrise, I often start by writing about the day’s cup—especially if I’m staying at someone else’s house. It’s fair to say I enjoy one of society’s acceptable addictions.

It wasn’t always like that. As a kid, I woke before the sun just to squeeze in extra time with my Barbies. My parents allowed it as long as I stayed quiet. But my favorite mornings were the ones my dad and I slipped out together before anyone else stirred. Those trips blur into one warm memory because they were always the same in the best way.

He’d nudge me awake, and I’d jump up in mismatched leggings and long underwear. Outside, our breath puffed into the cold—I still remember a poem I learned in school: Breathe and blow white clouds with every puff. It’s cold today, cold enough to see your breath, huff! Breathe dragon smoke today! We headed to his 1997 Ford Ranger, the heater whining and the engine’s rumble carrying through quiet streets in our small suburb.

At that hour, the 7-Eleven was always open, so we stopped for his coffee and my paper cup of hot chocolate. Out at the reservoir, a deer was already standing on the bank, still and luminous. I always got the small box of white powdered donuts—pure sugar, beautiful and messy, snowing sweetness on my clothes. My dad chose chocolate, which looked awful to me then.

We drove out of the neighborhood, up the winding road to the reservoir, with one more stop at the tackle shop, which opened early, too. My favorite thing to buy was a Styrofoam cup of worms: thick, glossy, wriggling in soft soil. I’d gently lift one and let it twist in my hands. I loved the neon dough baits too—bright green or pink that you could pinch into little balls. The jars of fish eggs, red and floating in gel, made me squirm. My dad’s metal tackle box held hooks and feathered lures that I thought looked pretty but boring to me. The messy bait felt real, and the fish seemed to agree.

Down on the dock, I set up beside him with my small pink tackle box and my yellow rod with a Snoopy reel. He helped me thread the line and skewer the worm. I learned to press the release and cast out, then wait for the faint pull that meant something was there. One dawn we froze as a deer looked back at us, still and luminous. The cooler held sodas, sandwiches, and the rest of the donuts. We didn’t talk much—that was the point.

Sometimes we went home with small catfish. Sometimes we tossed back bluegills. When the worms ran out, we used the neon bait. We never left without catching something. Even now, with coffee as my morning anchor, I chase that same quiet, that same warmth in my hands, the hush that taught me how to love the day before it really begins.
